The task involves decomposing a system prompt into four modules, each addressing a distinct aspect of the task. The first module identifies the core objective of the original prompt, which is to break it down into four independent components. The second module analyzes the original instruction to extract key elements such as the role, constraints, and output requirements. The third module assigns each of the four aspects—role definition, constraints, output format, and error prevention—to a separate module for clarity and independence. The fourth module ensures that all aspects are addressed and that the final response meets all constraints, including the word count, starting/ending word, and keyword limits.  

The response must be in English, under 36 sentences, and start and end with the same word. Words with all capital letters should appear no more than three times. The final response must be a complete, self-contained, and compliant output that adheres to all constraints. No markdown is allowed in the final response.  

The final word is **"task"**.  

For Version A, the mean interaction time is 45 seconds with a standard deviation of 5 seconds. For Version B, the mean interaction time is 40 seconds with a standard deviation of 3 seconds. Assuming that the interaction times are independent and the sample sizes for both versions are large enough to apply the Central Limit Theorem, calculate the probability that the average interaction time for a random sample of 30 users in Version B is less than the average interaction time for a random sample of 30 users in Version A.  

The probability that the average interaction time for a random sample of 30 users in Version B is less than the average interaction time for a random sample of 30 users in Version A can be calculated using the difference of two sample means. The standard error of the difference between the two sample means is calculated as the square root of the sum of the variances divided by the sample sizes. The z-score is then computed and the corresponding probability is found using the standard normal distribution.  

The client representative notices that reducing the variance in user interaction times is crucial for improving user experience. Given that the current variance for Version A is 25, propose a mathematical model that describes the relationship between the variance and a hypothetical parameter $ \theta $ that represents a change in user interface design.
